meta.notes: Proto-Germanic *hundaz; pre-Germanic prose; asterisk + macron in templates.

raw_kaikki.etymology_text:
From Middle English hound, from Old English hund, from Proto-West Germanic *hund, from Proto-Germanic *hundaz, from pre-Germanic *ḱuntós (compare Latvian sùnt-ene (“big dog”), enlargement of Proto-Indo-European *ḱwṓ (“dog”). Doublet of canine.
In 14th-century England, hound was the general word for all domestic canines, and dog referred to a subtype resembling the modern mastiff and bulldog. By the 16th century, dog had become the general word, and hound had begun to refer only to breeds used for hunting.

glosses: A dog, particularly a breed with a good sense of smell developed for hunting other animals.

examples.ref: 1996, Marc Parent, Turning Stones, Harcourt Brace & Company, →ISBN, page 93:
examples.text: On the way out of the building I was asked for my autograph. If I'd known who the signature hound thought I was, I would've signed appropriately.

examples.ref: 2004, Jodi Picoult, My Sister's Keeper, Simon & Schuster,, →ISBN, page 483:
examples.text: I still do not know if he's taken on this case because he's a glory hound, because he wants the PR, or if he simply wanted to help Anna.

examples.ref: 1915, Norman Duncan, "A Certain Recipient", in Harper's, volume 122, number 787, December 1915, republished in Harper's Monthly Magazine, volume 122, December 1915 to May 1916, page 108, "Are you alone, Goodson? […] I thought, perhaps, that the […] young woman, Goodson, who supplanted Mary?" […]
examples.text:
"She had a good many successors, John."
"You are such a hound, in that respect, Goodson," said Claywell, "and you have always been such a hound, that it astounds me to find you—unaccompanied."

glosses: A male who constantly seeks the company of desirable women.
raw_glosses: (by extension) A male who constantly seeks the company of desirable women.

examples.ref: c. 1608–1609 (date written), William Shakespeare, “The Tragedy of Coriolanus”, in Mr. William Shakespeares Comedies, Histories, & Tragedies […] (First Folio), London: […] Isaac Iaggard, and Ed[ward] Blount, published 1623, →OCLC, [Act V, scene vi]:
1888, Rudyard Kipling, “The Phantom Rickshaw”, in The Phantom 'Rickshaw and Other Tales, Allahabad: A.H. Wheeler and Co., page 12:
examples.text: I turned away and left her to finish her journey in peace, feeling, but only for a moment or two, that I had been an unutterably mean hound.

examples.ref: 1973, Elizabeth Walter, Come and Get Me and Other Uncanny Invitations:
examples.text: 'You blackmailing hound,' the parrot said distinctly, in what Hodges recognized as General Derby's voice. Anstruther turned pale.

definitions: A dog, particularly a breed with a good sense of smell developed for hunting other animals.
A male who constantly seeks the company of desirable women.
word_detail.etymology_text:
From Middle English hound, from Old English hund, from Proto-West Germanic *hund, from Proto-Germanic *hundaz, from pre-Germanic *ḱuntós (compare Latvian sùnt-ene (“big dog”), enlargement of Proto-Indo-European *ḱwṓ (“dog”). Doublet of canine.
In 14th-century England, hound was the general word for all domestic canines, and dog referred to a subtype resembling the modern mastiff and bulldog. By the 16th century, dog had become the general word, and hound had begun to refer only to breeds used for hunting.
